sections.title: Overview
sections.content: An acute dystonic reaction (ADR) is an extrapyramidal side effect characterised by sudden, involuntary abnormal postures or repetitive movements, resulting from sustained contractions of opposing muscle groups. It is caused by dopamine receptor blockade in the basal ganglia, which leads to a relative excess of cholinergic activity. ADR typically occurs within hours to days after the initiation or dose increase of causative medications, such as antipsychotics or antiemetics.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Laryngospasm (Potentially life-threatening) with stridor, dysphonia, throat pain, and/or dyspnoea
- Deviated eye gaze (upward/sideways), with or without eyelid spasm
- Torticollis (neck tightness and/or tilting/twisting)
- Opisthotonus (extreme back arching) with Arm flexion, leg extension and/or arched back
- Macroglossia, where the tongue feels enlarged, but is not, and protrudes from the mouth
- May present with trismus, dysarthria (slurred or slowing speech) and/or grimacing
- May involve hips, pelvis and abdominal wall muscles (tortipelvic crisis)
- Spasticity (softening and contraction) of trunk and limbs
- The patient will have normal mental status

**Other features may include:**
  - Anxiety
  - Agitation
  - Diaphoresis
  - Tachycardia
  - Tachypnoea

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Often an unusual reaction, but more common in young males, especially if prior history
- Can onset shortly after administration of medication but usually onsets hours to days later
- ADR may mimic several other conditions such as seizures, meningitis or hyperventilation, for example and is often misdiagnosed as anaphylaxis or seizures; focus on medication history and characteristic spasms
- History of Antipsychotic medication (haloperidol, risperidone and especially when used with young males), antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) antibiotics (erythromycin), antihistamines (ranitidine) or antidepressants (SSRI’s)

**Classifications to consider:**
  - Focal: Sudden involuntary contractions in one region (e.g., eyes, neck)
  - Segmental: Simultaneous involvement of two or more adjacent regions
  - Generalised: Widespread involvement; multiple body parts affected

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position for airway protection (upright, head tilted back, chin lifted, if laryngospasm is suspected)
- Consider oxygen
- Be prepared to assist ventilations if required
- Limb LEAD ECG (be aware of muscular artefact)

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider vascular access
- Consider fluid resuscitation
- Consider IM/IV Benzatropine (1-2mg) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider promethazine (25 mg to 50 mg IM) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider benzodiazepines in for persistent spasms in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Focal: Blepharospasm, Oculogyric crisis, spasmodic torticollis (wry neck)
- Segmental: Hemiballismus
- Generalised: Chorea, Tardive Dyskinesia
